    Sprint 2 – Agents & Agentic Systems

    📄

    Memory in agent systems

    Lesson 4

    Memory is a critical component in AI agent architectures. It enables agents to retain context from one interaction to the next, supporting multi-turn dialogues and adaptive behavior. Without memory, most AI agents would be stateless. This lesson will be a deep dive into how memory works in agent systems, covering short-term vs. long-term memory, practical implementation patterns, and emerging approaches like the Mem0 memory layer. The goal is to give you the understanding of how to build agents with robust memory, ensuring reasoning continuity in real-world applications.



    The Role of Memory in AI Agent Reasoning


    Memory is what allows AI agents (often powered by Large Language Models) to carry information forward through a dialogue or task. By remembering previous interactions, an agent can maintain and build up context, refer back to earlier facts and follow more complex chains of reasoning. For example, an agent with memory can recall a user’s preferences mentioned in an earlier query and use that to personalize future responses. In general, these are the main functionalities that memory enables:


    • Continuity: Memory provides continuity across turns. The agent’s responses can stay on topic and consistent with what has already been said, rather than treating each user query in isolation.

    • Learning and Adaptation: With memory, an agent can learn from feedback and adapt to user preferences over time. If a user corrects the agent or provides new information, the agent can store that and avoid repeating mistakes. You can think of it as a few-shot in-context learning as you hold the conversation.

    • Support for Reasoning: Many complex tasks require multi-step reasoning or referencing earlier steps. Memory allows intermediate results or decisions to be stored and used in later steps, enabling techniques like chain-of-thought reasoning in agents.

    • Personalization: Especially in user-facing assistants, remembering user-specific details (name, interests, past inquiries) is crucial for personalized, context-aware interactions. Without memory, each session would start from scratch with no personalization.


    In summary, memory turns a stateless LLM into a stateful agent that can hold an ongoing, contextual conversation. This leads to more efficient and natural interactions, rather than repetitive and disjointed responses. We will now break down the two broad categories of memory in agent systems: short-term and long-term memory.



    Short-Term vs. Long-Term Memory


    Short-term memory refers to the agent’s working context during an ongoing session or task. This typically means the conversation history or recently generated state that is kept in the loop for the LLM to draw context from. Short-term memory is thread-scoped, meaning it’s local to a single conversation or session (we are implementing it in the hands-on practice videos of this lesson). For example, in a chat application, short-term memory would include the recent dialogue turns between the user and the assistant.




    Long-term memory, on the other hand, is information retained across sessions. It’s a persistent store of knowledge or facts that the agent can recall even after the current conversation is over. Long-term memory might include a user’s profile information, facts learned in past conversations, or domain-specific knowledge the agent has accumulated. Unlike short-term memory, which resets with each new session/thread, long-term memory spans multiple sessions and is often shared across different conversation threads or even different users.


    Key differences of short-term and long-term memory:


    • Scope: Short-term memory is session-specific (ephemeral, within the active context window), whereas long-term memory is global or cross-session (persistent storage of knowledge).

    • Storage Mechanism: Short-term memory is usually maintained in-memory as a list of recent messages or state variables passed into the LLM’s context. Long-term memory is stored outside the immediate context, typically in a database, file, or vector index that persists beyond the runtime of a single session.

    • Access and Update: Short-term memory is directly included in the prompt to the LLM (so the model has access to it every turn, with some exceptions where specific pieces of the state would be left out to focus on desired outcomes). It’s updated continuously as the conversation progresses (appending new messages, replacing state). Long-term memory is not automatically in the prompt - the agent must retrieve relevant pieces of it when needed. Long-term memory might be updated on-demand (e.g. when the agent explicitly saves something important) or in batch (e.g. at the end of a session, summarizing and storing key points).

    • Size and Limitations: Short-term memory is limited by the LLM’s context window. Only a certain number of tokens can fit, meaning lengthy chat history might exceed this limit or degrade model performance. Long-term memory is theoretically unbounded in size (since it can live in external storage), but the challenge is finding the right information to bring into context when needed.


    Both types of memory work together to create an intelligent agent experience. Short-term memory gives local chat history while long-term memory provides global knowledge continuity. Let’s explore how to implement these in practice, using LangGraph as an example.



    Implementing Short-Term Memory


    Short-term memory is most commonly implemented as conversation history. In a basic scenario, each time the user sends a message, the agent includes that message plus some number of prior messages (including the assistant’s own replies) in the prompt for the LLM. Many frameworks provide abstractions to manage this message list for you. LangGraph, in particular, treats short-term conversation history as part of the agent’s state. In LangGraph, every agent can have a state that includes a list of messages (in the format expected by chat models - each message has a role and content). To enable the agent to remember these messages across turns, LangGraph uses a checkpointer to persist state between invocations. For example, you can use an in-memory checkpointer during development, or a database-backed checkpointer in production (we are implementing Postgres based checkpointer in our example videos), to save the conversation state:


    from langgraph.checkpoint.memory import InMemorySaver
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph

checkpointer = InMemorySaver()  # ephemeral storage of state (short-term memory)
workflow = StateGraph(...)
graph = workflow.compile(checkpointer=checkpointer)
# Now the graph will automatically save and load conversation state per thread


    In the above snippet, InMemorySaver will keep the conversation state in memory. In a real deployment, you would likely use a persistent checkpointer (e.g. PostgresSaver) so that if your service restarts or you want to resume a conversation later, the short-term memory isn’t lost. The checkpointer ties into the concept of a thread or session ID. When invoking the LangGraph agent, you provide a thread_id as part of the configuration, which tells the checkpointer which conversation’s state to load or update. This mechanism allows the agent to maintain separate short-term states for different conversations or users.


    Under the hood, each time the agent runs, LangGraph will append the latest user query and the assistant’s answer to the messages list in state, and the checkpointer will save this state associated with the thread ID. On the next turn, when the user sends another message, the state (including the full message history) is loaded and passed into the LLM prompt. This way, the agent can remember what was said earlier in the conversation.


    However, simply accumulating an ever-growing message list has downsides. As discussed earlier, LLMs have limited context windows and often degrade in performance when the prompt gets very large. Long conversations can cause the model to lose attention to important tokens or incur high latency and cost due to the many tokens. Therefore, a key aspect of short-term memory is management - deciding what to keep, what to discard, and what to compress. Some common short-term memory management strategies include:


    • Truncation (Dropping Old Messages): Only keep the most recent N messages in the prompt. Older messages are dropped once the history exceeds a certain length. This is a simple strategy to control context size, though important older information might be forgotten if not stored elsewhere.

    • Summarization: Replace long stretches of earlier dialogue with a concise summary. For example, after 50 messages, an agent could generate a summary of the conversation so far and then start a new context with that summary plus recent messages. This retains key info in a compact form.

    • Message Filtering: Omit or compress messages that are less relevant to the current query. For instance, if the conversation drifted through various topics, an agent might drop the off-topic segments when they are no longer needed. (Advanced implementations might use heuristic or model-based relevance scoring to decide this.)

    • Conditional Reset with Memory Storage: Conclude a topic or sub-dialogue by extracting any important facts from it into long-term memory, then clear the short-term memory and start fresh for a new topic.


    The optimal strategy often depends on the application - a customer support bot might summarize older parts of a conversation after an issue is resolved, whereas a storytelling agent might never drop narrative context until the story ends. In practice, implementing short-term memory in LangGraph is straightforward - include messages key in the state (which holds the messages list) of your graph, and use a checkpointer. The heavy lifting of storing, retrieving, and updating that message history is handled for you. The focus of developers should be on what goes into the messages (e.g., system prompts) and when to invoke memory management techniques like summarization or trimming. That’s why I find LangGraph so useful, it covers the plumbing part for us.



    Long-Term Memory and Retrieval Patterns


    While short-term memory deals with the immediate conversation, long-term memory provides an agent with a knowledge base that persists across conversations. This can range from simple user-specific facts to large collections of documents or past events. Long-term memory in agent systems is commonly implemented as some form of retrieval system - information is stored in a database or index, and when needed, the agent retrieves relevant pieces and injects them into the prompt.


    At its core, this is essentially a persistent document store (could be backed by a database, vector store, etc.) that lets you put, get, and search for memory entries. Each memory entry might be a JSON document or text blob representing something the agent should remember. Integrating retrieval into the agent loop is a crucial design decision. Here are a few common patterns for using long-term memory in an agent’s reasoning loop:


    • Pre-prompt Retrieval: On each new user query (or each conversation turn), the agent first searches the long-term memory store for any entries that might be relevant to the query. The query could be the user's message itself or some extracted keywords. Any matching memories are then added to the context (for example, as part of the system message or as additional context messages) before asking the LLM to respond. This pattern ensures the model is reminded of important facts from the past before it generates an answer. For example, if a user asks “What’s my name?”, The agent can query the store for an entry where the key might be “name” or where the content matches “name is”. If the memory “User name is Aurimas” was stored earlier, the search will retrieve it, and the agent can then prepend “(User info: name is Aurimas)” to the prompt so the model answers correctly.

    • On-Demand Tool Use: In a more dynamic agent (e.g. one that can use tools or reason about when to retrieve info), the agent might decide during its reasoning phase whether to retrieve from long-term memory (we essentially implemented this by hiding our vector database behind a tool). For example, using a framework like LangGraph, we could have a tool node or subgraph called search_memory that the agent can invoke if it determines that it lacks information currently in the context. The difference from pre-prompt retrieval is that the agent has autonomy to choose when to call the memory tool. In practice, this requires careful prompt engineering so the agent knows that this tool exists and when to use it.

    • Post-Response Memory Writing: After generating a response (or finishing a task), the agent might extract new facts or outcomes and store them into long-term memory for future use. This can be done synchronously in the flow or asynchronously. For example, if a user says “My name is Aurimas, please remember that”, the agent’s logic can detect the phrase remember and immediately save that fact to the memory store before even responding. Alternatively, an offline process might go through conversation logs and save important bits to memory without delaying the response to the user. The trade-off is usually between having the information available immediately and performance - writing synchronously ensures memory is up-to-date right away but could add overhead to the response time, whereas background updates might miss being immediately useful in the current conversation.

    • Memory Summarization and Consolidation: Over long periods, the memory store itself can grow large. Agents may employ strategies to summarize or consolidate long-term memory, similar to how we manage short-term context. For instance, if there are 100 log entries about a user’s behavior, the agent might condense that into a higher-level profile. For example, nightly jobs to summarize the day’s chats into key points.


    In general, long-term memory is also split into different types. This is a nice illustration depicting different types of memory available in agentic systems:


    Long-term:


    1. Episodic - This type of memory contains past interactions and actions performed by the agent. After an action is taken, the application controlling the agent would store the action in some kind of persistent storage so that it can be retrieved later if needed. A good example would be using a vector Database to store semantic meaning of the interactions.

    2. Semantic - Any external information that is available to the agent and any knowledge the agent should have about itself. You can think of this as a context similar to one used in RAG applications. It can be internal knowledge only available to the agent or a grounding context to isolate part of the internet scale data for more accurate answers.

    3. Procedural - This is systemic information like the structure of the System Prompt, available tools, guardrails etc. It will usually be stored in Git, Prompt and Tool Registries.


    Short-term:


    1. Occasionally, the agent application would pull information from long-term memory and store it locally if it is needed for the task at hand.

    2. All of the information pulled together from the long-term or stored in local memory is called short-term or working memory. Compiling all of it into a prompt will produce the prompt to be passed to the LLM and it will provide further actions to be taken by the system.



    Emerging Approaches: Mem0 and Memory as a Service Layer


    Up until now, we have discussed memory as something you implement within your agent or application, using tools like LangGraph’s state. Another upcoming trend is to treat memory as a first-class service or layer in the AI stack. One example of this is Mem0 - open-source modular memory layer for AI agents.


    Mem0 frames long-term memory as a modular service that can plug into any LLM-based application. The idea is that your AI agent (whether built with LangGraph, LangChain, or raw API calls) can outsource the problem of remembering to the Mem0 system. Mem0 provides a simple API to store and retrieve memories, along with advanced features like automatic memory compression, semantic linking, and personalized context management. Actually, Mem0 markets itself as a “self-improving memory layer” that makes AI interactions continuously smarter and more personalized. We will be looking into Mem0 next week.


    Key characteristics of the Mem0 approach:


    • Seamless Integration: Mem0 is designed to be framework-agnostic. You can use it with OpenAI API calls, LangGraph, CrewAI or others. This means you don’t have to deeply modify your agent code - you add Mem0 as a middleware that intercepts your prompts and augments them with memory and/or processes outputs to update memory.

    • Memory Compression and Efficiency: One of Mem0’s headline features is an intelligent memory compression engine. It automatically condenses chat histories into optimized summaries or vector representations, reducing the token footprint while preserving important context. According to Mem0, this can cut prompt token usage by up to 80% while retaining accurate conversation context. For a production system, this translates to cost savings and faster responses - the agent doesn’t need to send the entire chat history to the LLM, just the distilled memory.

    • Dynamic and Contextual Recall: Unlike basic RAG which might fetch from a static knowledge base, Mem0’s memory is continually updated and context-aware. It can relate entities across different interactions and prioritize recent and relevant information. I.e. Mem0 tries to always inject the most important facts (especially recent user mentions or corrections) into the prompt and can ignore or decay outdated information.

    • Multi-Type Memory (Semantic/Episodic): Mem0’s design draws inspiration from human memory systems by incorporating semantic memory (facts, knowledge) and episodic memory (event traces) into its model. For example, it might store a factual profile of the user (semantic) as well as logs of recent dialogues (episodic), and use both to formulate a response. Over time, it can deduce user preferences from repeated interactions and refine what it stores. This ability to consolidate and learn from the interaction history means the memory layer itself can improve personalization continuously.

    • Service Layer Abstraction: Importantly, Mem0 provides memory as a service – there's even a hosted version if you don’t want to manage the infrastructure. Your application code calls Mem0 to get context and to save new info, but Mem0 internally handles how that info is represented, indexed, and compressed. This encapsulation can speed up development (you leverage Mem0 implementation instead of reinventing memory management) and makes the memory component modular. If a better memory system comes along, in theory you could swap it out similarly to how you would swap a database technology, since your agent code just interfaces through an API.


    Mem0 is one of several cutting-edge efforts aiming to solve the long-term memory challenge for AI. Other projects, like MemoryBank, Ret-LLM, and HippoRAG, explore ideas such as simulating memory decay (forgetting curves) or neuromorphic memory models. For a developer or engineer, using Mem0 could mean writing significantly less custom code for memory. For example, rather than implementing a LangGraph store and custom retrieval logic, you might initialize Mem0 in one line and let it handle injection of past context. However, as with all “black box” systems, you lose control of how the memory is implemented.
